name: Ticketmaster
overview:
Ticketmaster is an event ticketing platform where users can browse events, select seats, and purchase tickets.
The system must handle massive traffic spikes when popular events go on sale (millions of users
simultaneously), prevent overselling (each seat sold exactly once), provide a fair queuing system, and process
payments securely. The seat selection and purchase flow requires strong consistency to prevent double-selling.

inScope: Browse events and view seat maps
Select and hold seats for 10 minutes during checkout
Prevent overselling (each seat sold exactly once)
Virtual waiting room for high-demand events

items: Support 100k concurrent users per event on sale without degradation
Seat reservation must be strongly consistent — each seat sold exactly once under any concurrency
Seat hold latency <200ms; reservation TTL enforced precisely at 10 minutes
99.99% availability during on-sale windows — downtime during a sale is catastrophic
Virtual waiting room must absorb traffic spikes from near-zero to 100k users in seconds
Seat map availability updates visible to users within 5 seconds of a hold or release

entities.name: Event
entities.schema:
{
  event_id: UUID,
  name: string,
  venue_id: string,
  date: timestamp,
  on_sale_time: timestamp,

  total_seats: int,
  available_seats: int
}

entities.name: Seat
entities.schema:
{
  seat_id: UUID,
  event_id: UUID,
  section: string,
  row: string,
  number: int,
  price: decimal,
  status: enum(AVAILABLE|HELD|SOLD)
}

entities.name: Reservation
entities.schema:
{
  reservation_id: UUID,
  user_id: string,
  seat_ids: [UUID],
  expires_at: timestamp,  // 10 minutes from creation
  status: enum(ACTIVE|COMPLETED|EXPIRED)
}

endpoints.response:
{ reservation_id, expires_at }
Response 409: { error: 'Seats not available', unavailable_seat_ids }

approaches.title: Optimistic Locking
approaches.description: Read seat status, check if AVAILABLE, update to HELD with version check. Under high concurrency (100k users competing for 1000 seats), most transactions fail and retry. Retry storms overwhelm the DB.
approaches.tradeoff: Retry storms under high concurrency; poor performance.

approaches.title: Pessimistic Locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE)
approaches.description: Lock seat rows during reservation. Only one transaction can hold the lock at a time. Correct but serializes all reservations. At 100k concurrent users, lock wait times become unacceptable.
approaches.tradeoff: Serialized access; high lock wait times under load.

approaches.title: Redis Atomic Seat Reservation
approaches.description: Store seat status in Redis. Use a Lua script to atomically check-and-set seat status from AVAILABLE to HELD. Redis handles 100k ops/sec. The Lua script is atomic (no race conditions). Held seats have a TTL of 10 minutes (auto-release on expiry). Seat status is asynchronously persisted to PostgreSQL via Kafka. Redis is the source of truth during the sale.
approaches.tradeoff: Redis failure requires fallback; async persistence means brief inconsistency window.

approaches.title: No Queue (First Come First Served)
approaches.description: Allow all users to access the ticketing page simultaneously. 100k concurrent users overwhelm the seat reservation system. Users with faster internet connections have an unfair advantage.
approaches.tradeoff: System overload; unfair to users on slower connections.

approaches.title: Virtual Waiting Room with Position Queue
approaches.description: When an event goes on sale, users are placed in a virtual waiting room. A queue service assigns each user a random position. Users are admitted to the ticketing page in batches (e.g., 1000 users every 30 seconds). Randomized queue position is fairer than first-come-first-served. The ticketing system only handles 1000 concurrent users at a time, preventing overload. Users receive a JWT token when admitted; the token is required to access the seat selection page.
approaches.tradeoff: Users may wait a long time; queue position randomization may feel unfair to users who joined

components.name: Event Service
components.responsibility: Manages event and seat inventory; serves seat maps

components.name: Waiting Room Service
components.responsibility: Manages virtual queue; admits users in batches; issues admission tokens

components.name: Reservation Service
components.responsibility: Handles seat holds; uses Redis Lua scripts for atomic reservation

components.name: Ticket Service
components.responsibility: Generates tickets (QR codes) after successful payment

components.name: Redis
components.responsibility: Seat status (AVAILABLE/HELD/SOLD); waiting room queue; reservation

components.name: PostgreSQL
components.responsibility: Persistent seat inventory; order history; ticket records

components.name: Kafka
components.responsibility: Seat status changes for async persistence; payment events

questions.title: 2. Reservation Expiry
questions.content:
Held seats are automatically released after 10 minutes if not purchased. Redis TTL handles this automatically:
when the TTL expires, the seat status key is deleted, making the seat available again. A Kafka event is

published when a reservation expires, triggering a notification to the user and updating the PostgreSQL seat
status. The seat map polling picks up the newly available seats within 5 seconds.

questions.title: 3. Handling the On-Sale Spike
questions.content:
When a popular event goes on sale, traffic spikes from near-zero to 100k concurrent users in seconds. The
system handles this with: (1) Auto-scaling: API servers scale out based on CPU/request rate, (2) CDN caching:
the event page and seat map are cached at CDN edge, (3) Virtual waiting room: limits concurrent users to
1000, (4) Redis cluster: pre-scaled for the expected load, (5) Read replicas: seat availability queries go to
PostgreSQL read replicas.

questions.title: 4. Ticket Generation
questions.content:
After successful payment, the Ticket Service generates a unique QR code for each ticket. The QR code
encodes: ticket_id, event_id, seat_id, and a cryptographic signature (HMAC-SHA256 with a secret key). The
venue's access control system validates the QR code by verifying the signature and checking the ticket_id
against the database. This prevents ticket forgery.

questions.title: 5. Scalability for Multiple Simultaneous Events
questions.content:
Ticketmaster handles thousands of events simultaneously. Each event's seat inventory is isolated in Redis (key
prefix = event_id). Events with high demand get dedicated Redis instances. The Waiting Room Service is
event-specific: each event has its own queue. This isolation prevents one popular event from affecting others.

purchase

GET seat map
keyFlows: ① User joins virtual waiting room → admitted in batches (1000/30s)
② User selects seats → Reservation Svc → Redis Lua (atomic HELD)
③ Held seats have 10-min TTL → auto-release on expiry
④ User purchases → Payment Svc → Ticket Svc generates QR code
⑤ Seat map served from CDN; availability polled every 5s

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study Redis TTL-based expiration: how Redis handles key expiration (lazy expiration + active
expiration), and how to use keyspace notifications to trigger actions on key expiry.
Expert Note: Learn about virtual waiting room design: how Ticketmaster and other ticketing platforms
implement fair queuing, the trade-off between randomized and FIFO queue positions.
Expert Note: Understand seat map rendering: how SVG-based seat maps work, how to efficiently update
seat availability for thousands of seats without re-rendering the entire map.
Expert Note: Study HMAC-SHA256 for ticket authentication: how to generate and verify cryptographic
signatures, and why this is more secure than storing ticket validity in a database.
Expert Note: Learn about the thundering herd problem in ticketing: how millions of users simultaneously
hitting a sale page can overwhelm backend systems, and how CDN caching and rate limiting mitigate this.
